title: Sue Black: I think of death as female
gist: Forensic anthropologist and crossbench peer Sue Black discusses her ease with death, her career identifying victims in war zones and disasters, and — most substantively — warns that England and Wales's marketised forensic science sector is in an 'irretrievable graveyard spiral' that is producing wrongful convictions, urging that forensic science stay institutionally independent from the police.

themes.theme: UK forensic science system in crisis

insights.claim: A 2025 parliamentary Westminster report found England and Wales's forensic science sector in an 'almost irretrievable graveyard spiral,' concluding that the shift to a commercial, marketised provision system created a monopoly that has led to poor police investigations, more unsolved crimes and more wrongful convictions.
insights.speaker: Sue Black
entities: House of Lords Science and Technology Committee
insights.so_what: A documented, parliament-commissioned finding of systemic failure in a privatised public-service market — relevant precedent for evaluating other marketised UK public services and for any investors/vendors in the forensic-services procurement space.
insights.quote: poor police investigations, increasing unsolved crimes and more wrongful convictions
insights.locator: The 2025 Westminster report, commissioned by parliament

insights.claim: The House of Lords Science and Technology Committee conducted a follow-up review and independently agreed with the Westminster report's conclusion that the forensic science system is 'broken,' giving the finding cross-institutional corroboration.
insights.speaker: Sue Black
entities: House of Lords Science and Technology Committee
insights.so_what: Two separate parliamentary bodies reaching the same conclusion raises the likelihood of near-term legislative or structural intervention in UK forensic services.
insights.quote: The system is not working for anybody.
insights.locator: We had created the Westminster report to look at it

insights.claim: A UK government white paper published in January 2026 proposes creating a National Forensic Service (NFS) under a single centralised police force to fix efficiency and standardise best practice, but Sue Black — who helped author the original critique — doubts England and Wales will actually adopt this model, unlike Scotland and Northern Ireland, where forensic services already sit in the public sector and are wholly separate from the police.
insights.speaker: Sue Black
insights.so_what: Signals continued policy uncertainty/delay in UK forensic services reform, and flags a specific structural risk (embedding forensic science inside police forces) that Black views as likely to worsen, not fix, the wrongful-conviction problem.
insights.quote: I [don't] think it's the way we're going to go in England and Wales.
insights.locator: A government white paper in January this year proposed

insights.claim: Black argues the core problem is not scientific quality but governance and independence: a forensic scientist employed directly by the police would inevitably feel partisan even with the best intentions, so the priority reform should be maintaining a strict, 'sterile clinical corridor' separating forensic science from both the police and the prosecution.
insights.speaker: Sue Black
insights.so_what: A concrete institutional-design principle (independence of forensic science from policing) that should be a litmus test for evaluating any forthcoming UK forensic-service reform proposal.
insights.quote: it's not the science that's the problem, it's the bureaucracy and the way in which it is governed.
insights.locator: Will forensic scientists be able to remain impartial

insights.claim: Black's method for identifying individuals in photographs from the vein patterns on their hands formed part of the evidence that led to the conviction of members of Scotland's largest paedophile ring, illustrating a forensic biometric technique with direct courtroom impact.
insights.speaker: Sue Black
insights.so_what: A concrete example of a niche forensic biometric technique (hand vein-pattern ID) with demonstrated evidentiary value in major criminal prosecutions.
insights.locator: Some years earlier, her method for identifying people in photographs

insights.claim: Black led forensic identification work in major conflict and disaster zones, including exhuming mass graves to identify victims of Serbian troops after the 1998 Kosovo war (for which she received an OBE), and identifying victims of the 2004 Boxing Day tsunami in Thailand, alongside consulting work in Iraq, Sierra Leone and Grenada for the Foreign and Commonwealth Office and the United Nations.
insights.speaker: Sue Black
insights.so_what: Establishes the institutional credibility (UN, FCO-level engagements) behind her critique of the current UK forensic science governance model.
insights.locator: Her work has taken her to conflict zones around the world

themes.theme: Personal philosophy on death and compartmentalisation

insights.claim: Black plans to have her own body dissected after death not by medical students but by trainee anatomists, and then wants her skeleton to become a teaching specimen at the University of Dundee's anatomy department, reflecting a career-long comfort with death she traces to skinning game with her father from around age five.
insights.speaker: Sue Black
insights.so_what: Illustrates how sustained early and professional exposure can normalise attitudes toward death that differ sharply from typical public discomfort — relevant context for understanding end-of-life-industry and body-donation attitudes.
insights.quote: From the age of five, I'm up to my elbows in blood.
insights.locator: Her ease with death and dissection started at a young age

insights.claim: Black manages the psychological toll of decades of trauma-exposed forensic work by strictly compartmentalising — using a mental 'clinical box' she opens only at work and closes when she leaves — while acknowledging that every case involving victims, especially children, 'lives with you' and cannot fully be unseen.
insights.speaker: Sue Black
insights.so_what: A specific coping framework used by professionals in chronic-trauma-exposed fields (forensics, medicine, war-crimes investigation), useful context for occupational mental-health approaches in these sectors.
insights.quote: I've always tried to have a clinical box inside my head. When I go to work, I go in there and close the door behind me and I do everything I need to do.
insights.locator: Black tries not to let her professional life

top_entities: House of Lords Science and Technology Committee
